lessons.title: IAM Overview
lessons.overview: Understand IAM's dual role as AWS's authentication and authorization service — managing who can access what, from the root user and IAM users to EC2 instance profiles, S3 bucket policies, and the principle of least privilege.
lessons.overviewVi: Hiểu vai trò kép của IAM với tư cách là dịch vụ xác thực và phân quyền của AWS — quản lý ai được truy cập vào gì, từ root user và IAM users đến EC2 instance profiles, S3 bucket policies và nguyên tắc least privilege.

sections.label: Overview
sections.heading: Authentication and Authorization in AWS
sections.contentHtml:
AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) has two primary functions that mirror its name:

Identity (Authentication)Identifies who the validated user or entity is — IAM user, group, or role
Access Management (Authorization)Controls what that identity is allowed to do via policies

When you create a new AWS account, the root user is automatically created. It is accessed with the email and password used to create the account and has unrestricted access — including account settings, billing, and account closure.

Best practice: Do not use the root user for daily tasks. Create IAM users and follow the least privilege principle.
sections.contentVi: AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) có hai chức năng chính: Identity (Xác thực) — xác định AI là người dùng hợp lệ (IAM user, group, role); Access Management (Phân quyền) — kiểm soát người dùng đó ĐƯỢC LÀM GÌ thông qua policies. Khi tạo tài khoản AWS mới, root user được tạo tự động. Root user truy cập bằng email và mật khẩu đăng ký, có quyền không giới hạn — kể cả cài đặt tài khoản, thanh toán và đóng tài khoản. Thực hành tốt nhất: không dùng root user cho công việc hàng ngày, tạo IAM users và áp dụng nguyên tắc least privilege.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: Least Privilege and Policy Types
sections.contentHtml:
The principle of least privilege states that you grant only the permissions strictly necessary for an entity to perform its designated task — nothing more.

AWS Managed PolicyPre-built by AWS, aligned to common job functions (Administrator, Developer, SecurityAuditor). Read-only — you cannot edit it.
Customer Managed PolicyCreated and managed by you in your account. Fully customizable, can be attached to multiple identities.
Inline PolicyEmbedded directly in a single user/group/role. Deleted automatically when the identity is deleted. Strict one-to-one relationship.
Example of least privilege: A new DevOps engineer who only needs CloudFormation access should be added to a group with a CloudFormation-only policy — NOT given PowerUserAccess or AdministratorAccess.
sections.contentVi: Nguyên tắc least privilege: chỉ cấp đúng quyền cần thiết cho thực thể thực hiện nhiệm vụ của nó — không thừa không thiếu. AWS Managed Policy — được AWS tạo sẵn, theo job functions phổ biến (Administrator, Developer, SecurityAuditor), chỉ đọc, không chỉnh sửa được. Customer Managed Policy — bạn tự tạo và quản lý, tùy chỉnh hoàn toàn, có thể gắn vào nhiều identity. Inline Policy — nhúng trực tiếp vào một user/group/role, tự xóa khi identity bị xóa, quan hệ một-một. Ví dụ: DevOps engineer chỉ cần CloudFormation nên được thêm vào group với CloudFormation-only policy — KHÔNG cấp PowerUserAccess hoặc AdministratorAccess.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: IAM for AWS Services and Resources
sections.contentHtml:
IAM isn't just for human users — it also controls what AWS services can do:

EC2 Instance ProfilePasses an IAM role to an EC2 instance so applications running on it can access other AWS services (e.g. upload to S3) without storing credentials
S3 Bucket PolicyResource-based policy that grants IAM users in other AWS accounts access to the bucket — enables cross-account S3 sharing
IAM DB Authentication (RDS/Aurora)Use IAM to manage database access without storing a DB password — the EC2 instance uses its instance profile to authenticate to RDS
SQS Access PolicyResource-based policy on an SQS queue that grants external accounts or companies permission to poll the queue
sections.contentVi: IAM không chỉ dành cho người dùng — nó còn kiểm soát những gì dịch vụ AWS có thể làm. EC2 Instance Profile — truyền IAM role vào EC2 instance để ứng dụng chạy trên đó truy cập AWS services khác (như upload S3) mà không cần lưu credentials. S3 Bucket Policy — policy theo tài nguyên cho phép IAM users từ tài khoản AWS khác truy cập bucket, kích hoạt cross-account S3 sharing. IAM DB Authentication (RDS/Aurora) — dùng IAM quản lý truy cập database mà không lưu DB password, EC2 instance dùng instance profile để xác thực với RDS. SQS Access Policy — policy trên SQS queue cho phép tài khoản hoặc công ty bên ngoài poll queue.

sections.label: Exam Focus
sections.heading: Identity-Based vs Resource-Based Policies
sections.contentHtml:
• Identity-based policy — attached to an IAM user, group, or role. Controls what that identity CAN DO.
  • Resource-based policy — attached to a resource (S3 bucket, SQS queue, Lambda, etc.). Controls who CAN ACCESS the resource. Enables cross-account access.
  • Permissions boundary — sets the MAXIMUM permissions an identity-based policy can grant. Does not grant permissions itself.
  • Root user — has full unrestricted access. Cannot be restricted by SCPs. Should only be used for account-level tasks (billing, account closure, support plan changes).
Exam pattern: If a scenario mentions needing cross-account access to a specific resource, the answer usually involves a resource-based policy (e.g. S3 bucket policy, SQS access policy) OR a cross-account IAM role.
sections.contentVi: Identity-based policy — gắn vào IAM user, group hoặc role; kiểm soát identity đó CÓ THỂ LÀM GÌ. Resource-based policy — gắn vào tài nguyên (S3 bucket, SQS queue, Lambda,...); kiểm soát AI CÓ THỂ TRUY CẬP tài nguyên; cho phép cross-account access. Permissions boundary — đặt giới hạn TỐI ĐA quyền mà identity-based policy có thể cấp; không tự cấp quyền. Root user — có quyền không giới hạn, không bị SCPs hạn chế, chỉ dùng cho tác vụ cấp tài khoản (thanh toán, đóng tài khoản, thay đổi support plan). Pattern thi: scenario cần cross-account access đến resource cụ thể → thường dùng resource-based policy (S3 bucket policy, SQS access policy) HOẶC cross-account IAM role.

summary: IAM manages authentication (who you are) and authorization (what you can do) for all AWS identities and resources
Root user has unrestricted access — never use for daily tasks; create IAM users and follow least privilege
Three policy types: AWS Managed (pre-built, read-only), Customer Managed (custom, reusable), Inline (embedded, deleted with the identity)
Identity-based policies attach to users/groups/roles; resource-based policies attach to resources (S3, SQS) and enable cross-account access
EC2 Instance Profile passes an IAM role to EC2 — applications use temporary credentials instead of storing long-term access keys
summaryVi: IAM quản lý xác thực (bạn là ai) và phân quyền (bạn được làm gì) cho tất cả identity và tài nguyên AWS
Root user có quyền không giới hạn — không dùng cho công việc hàng ngày; tạo IAM users và áp dụng least privilege
Ba loại policy: AWS Managed (có sẵn, chỉ đọc), Customer Managed (tùy chỉnh, tái sử dụng được), Inline (nhúng trực tiếp, xóa cùng identity)
Identity-based policy gắn vào user/group/role; resource-based policy gắn vào tài nguyên (S3, SQS) và cho phép cross-account access
EC2 Instance Profile truyền IAM role vào EC2 — ứng dụng dùng temporary credentials thay vì lưu long-term access keys

quiz.question: A company's security team wants to ensure that no individual user has more AWS permissions than strictly necessary for their job function. A new data analyst needs read-only access to Amazon S3 and Amazon Athena. Which approach BEST implements the principle of least privilege?
options: Attach the AWS managed AdministratorAccess policy to the user
Attach the AWS managed PowerUserAccess policy to the user
Create a customer managed policy granting only S3 read and Athena query actions, then attach it to the user
Give the user the root user credentials with instructions to only use S3 and Athena
quiz.explanation: A customer managed policy granting only S3 read and Athena query actions is the most precise implementation of least privilege — the user gets exactly what they need and nothing more. AdministratorAccess and PowerUserAccess grant far more permissions than required, violating least privilege. Sharing root credentials is a critical security violation — root has unrestricted access to all services and account settings.

quiz.question: An application running on an Amazon EC2 instance needs to upload processed files to an Amazon S3 bucket and write metrics to Amazon CloudWatch. The security team prohibits storing long-term credentials on EC2 instances. Which solution BEST meets these requirements?
options: Store the IAM access key and secret access key in the EC2 instance's /etc/environment file
Create an IAM user, generate access keys, and embed them in the application's configuration file
Attach an IAM role with S3 and CloudWatch permissions to the EC2 instance using an instance profile
Use the root user access keys and pass them as environment variables to the application
quiz.explanation: An IAM role attached via an instance profile provides temporary, automatically rotated credentials to the EC2 instance — no long-term credentials are stored. Applications retrieve credentials from the EC2 instance metadata service. Storing access keys in files or environment variables creates a security risk if the instance is compromised. Root user access keys should never be created or used.

quiz.question: A company has two AWS accounts: Account A owns an S3 bucket with financial reports. Account B's analysts need read access to that bucket. Which mechanism enables cross-account access to the S3 bucket with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Create IAM users in Account A and share the credentials with Account B's analysts
Add an S3 bucket policy in Account A that grants read permissions to Account B's IAM roles
Enable S3 Cross-Region Replication to copy objects to a bucket in Account B
Create a VPC peering connection between Account A and Account B
quiz.explanation: An S3 bucket policy (resource-based policy) in Account A that grants access to Account B's IAM roles enables cross-account access without sharing credentials. Account B's users assume their roles to access the bucket directly. Sharing IAM credentials is a security violation. S3 replication copies data — it doesn't control access and doubles storage costs. VPC peering connects networks — it doesn't grant S3 access permissions.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is reviewing IAM configurations for a new project. One IAM policy is attached directly to a single IAM user and will be deleted automatically if that user is deleted. Another policy is attached to an IAM group and can be reused across multiple users. Which policy types are these, respectively?
options: Both are AWS managed policies
Inline policy; customer managed policy
Customer managed policy; AWS managed policy
Session policy; permissions boundary
quiz.explanation: An inline policy has a strict one-to-one relationship with its identity and is automatically deleted when the identity is deleted. A customer managed policy is standalone (not tied to a specific identity), can be attached to multiple users/groups/roles, and persists independently. AWS managed policies are pre-built by AWS and can't be edited. Session policies limit session permissions and are not attached to identities permanently.

quiz.question: A company uses Amazon RDS for its application database. The security team wants to eliminate hardcoded database passwords in application code running on EC2. They want EC2 instances to authenticate to RDS using their AWS identity. Which feature enables this?
options: AWS Secrets Manager with automatic rotation
IAM Database Authentication for RDS
RDS encryption at rest with KMS
VPC security groups restricting RDS inbound access to the EC2 subnet
quiz.explanation: IAM Database Authentication allows EC2 instances to authenticate to RDS using an IAM role (via instance profile) instead of a database username and password. The application generates an auth token from IAM and uses it to connect — no password is stored in code. Secrets Manager also eliminates hardcoded passwords but still uses a password internally (just stored in Secrets Manager). KMS encryption protects data at rest but doesn't change authentication. Security groups control network access, not authentication.

quiz.question: An external logistics company needs to send messages to an SQS queue owned by a retail company without having an IAM user in the retail company's AWS account. Which mechanism grants the logistics company access to the queue?
options: Create a VPN connection between both companies and use AWS Direct Connect
Add a resource-based SQS access policy to the queue granting the external company's AWS account permission to SendMessage
Create an IAM user in the retail company's account and share credentials with the logistics company
Enable SQS cross-region replication to push messages to the logistics company's account
quiz.explanation: An SQS access policy (resource-based policy) on the queue grants the external company's AWS account permission to send messages without creating IAM users in the retail account. The logistics company uses their own IAM credentials to make the SQS API call. Sharing IAM credentials is a security violation. VPN/Direct Connect is for network connectivity, not queue access permissions. SQS doesn't have a cross-region replication feature.

quiz.question: A cloud administrator needs to perform an AWS task that requires root user access — specifically, changing the AWS account's support plan. They cannot find the root credentials. Which statement about the root user is MOST accurate?
options: Any IAM administrator can perform root-level tasks by assuming a special root IAM role
The root user is the account's original sign-in email address and password — it cannot be replaced by any IAM user regardless of permissions
An IAM user with AdministratorAccess can change the support plan the same as the root user
AWS Support can reset root access and temporarily grant another IAM user root-level permissions
quiz.explanation: The AWS account root user is uniquely identified by the account's original email address and password. Certain tasks (changing support plan, closing account, canceling Reserved Instances) can only be performed by the root user — no IAM user, regardless of their policy, can perform these root-only tasks. There is no 'root IAM role' that can be assumed. AWS Support cannot grant root-level permissions to other IAM users.

quiz.question: A security architect is evaluating IAM policies for an application. Which TWO statements about permissions boundaries are correct? (Choose TWO)
options: A permissions boundary sets the maximum permissions an identity-based policy can grant to an IAM user or role
A permissions boundary by itself grants permissions to an IAM user
If a permissions boundary does not allow an action, that action is denied even if an identity-based policy explicitly allows it
Permissions boundaries apply to AWS Organizations accounts and replace SCPs
Permissions boundaries are a type of resource-based policy
quiz.explanation: Permissions boundaries define the ceiling of permissions — an identity can only perform actions allowed by BOTH the identity-based policy AND the permissions boundary. A permissions boundary does not grant permissions on its own (Option B is wrong). It is not the same as an SCP (SCPs apply at the organization level, not the identity level — Option D is wrong). Permissions boundaries are a type of IAM policy applied to identities, not resources (Option E is wrong).

quiz.question: A company manages multiple AWS accounts across departments. Each department's S3 bucket needs to be shared with all other departments. The IAM team wants the LEAST operational overhead solution. Which approach BEST enables this cross-account S3 sharing?
options: Create IAM users in each account and distribute credentials to all departments
Set up S3 bucket policies in each account granting read access to all department AWS account IDs
Enable S3 Object Lock on all shared buckets
Use Amazon CloudFront to serve S3 content publicly to all departments
quiz.explanation: S3 bucket policies (resource-based policies) can grant access to specific AWS account IDs, enabling cross-account access without distributing credentials. Each department's IAM users use their own credentials to access buckets in other accounts. Distributing credentials is a security violation. S3 Object Lock prevents object deletion/modification — it doesn't manage cross-account access. CloudFront makes content publicly accessible — it bypasses IAM-based access control entirely.

quiz.question: A developer accidentally attached an overly permissive inline policy to an IAM user granting full S3 access. The security team removes the IAM user entirely to revoke access quickly. What happens to the inline policy?
options: The inline policy remains in the IAM console and can be reattached to another user
The inline policy is automatically deleted when the IAM user is deleted
The inline policy is moved to a deleted-policies archive in IAM
The inline policy is converted to an AWS managed policy for future use
quiz.explanation: Inline policies maintain a strict one-to-one relationship with their associated identity — they are automatically deleted when the identity (user, group, or role) is deleted. This is a key difference from managed policies, which exist independently and persist after the identity is deleted. Inline policies cannot be reattached because they don't exist as standalone policy objects.

quiz.question: A company wants to give a contractor limited access to their AWS environment. The contractor should only have permissions to read from specific DynamoDB tables and invoke specific Lambda functions — nothing else. Which policy type provides the MOST granular, scoped control with the least risk of permissions leakage?
options: AWS managed ReadOnlyAccess policy attached to the contractor's IAM user
A customer managed policy listing specific DynamoDB read actions and Lambda InvokeFunction for specific resource ARNs
The AWS managed PowerUserAccess policy with an inline deny for EC2 and S3
A permissions boundary that restricts to DynamoDB and Lambda
quiz.explanation: A customer managed policy specifying exact API actions (DynamoDB GetItem, Query, etc.) scoped to specific table ARNs and Lambda InvokeFunction on specific function ARNs is the most granular approach — it grants exactly what is needed. AWS managed ReadOnlyAccess grants read access to ALL services, which violates least privilege. PowerUserAccess with inline deny still leaves many services accessible. A permissions boundary alone doesn't grant any permissions — it must be paired with an identity-based policy.

quiz.question: An architect reviewing IAM configuration notes that an EC2 instance is currently calling the AWS CLI with access keys embedded in a user-data script. The security team flags this as a vulnerability. What is the MOST secure remediation?
options: Move the access keys to an environment variable on the EC2 instance instead of user-data
Encrypt the access keys using AWS KMS before embedding them in the user-data script
Remove the embedded access keys and attach an IAM role to the EC2 instance via an instance profile
Store the access keys in AWS Secrets Manager and retrieve them at runtime from user-data
quiz.explanation: An IAM role attached via an instance profile provides temporary, automatically rotated credentials that the EC2 instance retrieves from the instance metadata service — no long-term credentials are ever stored on the instance. Moving keys to environment variables still stores long-term credentials on the instance. Encrypting keys with KMS still leaves them on the instance. Secrets Manager is better than hardcoding but still requires an initial credential to authenticate to Secrets Manager — an instance profile is the correct architectural solution.

quiz.question: A startup is designing their first multi-developer AWS environment. They want one policy that grants S3 full access and will be shared among all developers. If a developer leaves, revoking their S3 access should not require modifying the policy itself. Which approach meets these requirements?
options: Create an inline S3 full access policy and attach it to each developer's IAM user individually
Create a customer managed S3 full access policy, attach it to an IAM group, and add developers to the group
Create a separate AWS managed policy for each developer
Attach the root user's policy to all developer IAM users
quiz.explanation: A customer managed policy attached to an IAM group allows all group members to inherit the policy. Revoking a developer's access is as simple as removing them from the group — the policy itself is unchanged. Inline policies attached individually require editing each user. AWS managed policies are created by AWS and cannot be customized. Root user credentials should never be shared.

quiz.question: A company's IAM administrator attached an AWS managed policy to a developer's IAM user. The administrator later tries to edit the policy to add a new permission but finds they cannot modify it. Why?
options: The policy is currently attached to a user, so it becomes read-only
AWS managed policies are created and maintained by AWS and cannot be edited by customers
The administrator needs root user access to edit any IAM policy
The policy is an inline policy embedded in the user and requires user deletion to modify
quiz.explanation: AWS managed policies are created and maintained by AWS — customers can attach or detach them but cannot modify their contents. To add custom permissions, the administrator should create a customer managed policy with the desired permissions. Policies don't become read-only due to attachment status. Root access is not required to create and edit customer managed policies. The policy described is a managed policy, not an inline policy.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is reviewing an application's IAM configuration. The application runs on EC2 and needs to access S3. Which TWO statements represent security best practices? (Choose TWO)
options: Attach an IAM role to the EC2 instance profile — the application retrieves temporary credentials from instance metadata
Generate an IAM user access key pair and store it in the application's config file on the EC2 instance
Grant the EC2 role only the specific S3 actions and bucket ARNs it needs (least privilege)
Use the root account access keys for the application since root has guaranteed access to S3
Create a single IAM policy with S3:* on * to avoid future permission issues
quiz.explanation: Using an instance profile with an IAM role (Option A) provides temporary, auto-rotated credentials without storing long-term credentials. Scoping permissions to only necessary actions and specific resource ARNs (Option C) enforces least privilege. Storing access keys in config files (Option B) is a security risk. Root access keys should never be created or used for applications (Option D). Granting S3:* on * violates least privilege and gives the application far more access than needed (Option E).

lessons.title: IAM Identities
lessons.overview: Master the three IAM identity types — users, groups, and roles — their key differences in credential types and use cases, and the critical distinction between long-term user credentials and temporary role-based credentials.
lessons.overviewVi: Nắm vững ba loại IAM identity — users, groups và roles — sự khác biệt về loại credentials và trường hợp sử dụng, cùng sự phân biệt quan trọng giữa long-term user credentials và temporary role-based credentials.

sections.label: Overview
sections.heading: The Three IAM Identity Types
sections.contentHtml:
IAM provides three identity types, each serving a different purpose:

IAM UserRepresents an actual person or a service. Has long-term credentials (password + access key pair).
IAM GroupA collection of IAM users. Policies attached to the group are inherited by all members. Simplifies bulk permission management.
IAM RoleAn identity assumed by AWS services, other accounts, or federated users. Provides temporary security credentials. No long-term credentials.
sections.contentVi: IAM cung cấp ba loại identity, mỗi loại phục vụ một mục đích khác nhau: IAM User — đại diện cho người thực hoặc service, có long-term credentials (password + access key pair). IAM Group — tập hợp IAM users, policy gắn vào group được kế thừa bởi tất cả thành viên, đơn giản hóa quản lý quyền hàng loạt. IAM Role — identity được assume bởi AWS services, tài khoản khác hoặc federated users, cung cấp temporary security credentials, không có long-term credentials.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: IAM User and Group Rules
sections.contentHtml:
An IAM user consists of three components:

  • A name (username for console login)
  • A password (for AWS Management Console)
  • An access key pair (Access Key ID + Secret Access Key) for CLI, API, SDK, and CDK programmatic access

Key rules for IAM groups:

  • A group can contain multiple users; a single user can belong to multiple groups
  • Groups cannot be nested — a group cannot contain another group, only IAM users
  • There is no default group that automatically includes all users — you must create and manage group membership manually
  • Groups don't have credentials — only users and roles do
Exam tip: IAM groups cannot be nested. This is a common distractor in exam questions.
sections.contentVi: IAM user gồm ba thành phần: tên (username đăng nhập console), mật khẩu (cho AWS Management Console), cặp access key (Access Key ID + Secret Access Key) để truy cập CLI, API, SDK và CDK. Quy tắc IAM groups: group có thể chứa nhiều user; một user có thể thuộc nhiều group; groups KHÔNG THỂ lồng nhau — group không chứa group khác, chỉ chứa IAM users; không có default group tự động bao gồm tất cả users — phải tự tạo và quản lý thành viên; groups không có credentials — chỉ users và roles mới có. Mẹo thi: IAM groups không thể lồng nhau — đây là distractor phổ biến trong đề thi.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: IAM Role: Temporary Credentials and Types
sections.contentHtml:
An IAM role is similar to an IAM user but with critical differences:

No long-term credentialsNo password, no permanent access keys. Provides temporary security credentials for the duration of the assumed session.
Multiple principals can assume itUnlike an IAM user (tied to one person), a role can be assumed by an EC2 instance, a Lambda function, an ECS task, and others simultaneously

Types of IAM roles:

Cross-Account RoleGrants access to resources in one account to a trusted principal in a different AWS account — the primary method for multi-account architectures
AWS Service RoleA role an AWS service (like EC2, Lambda) assumes to perform actions in YOUR account on your behalf. Scoped to your account only.
AWS Service-Linked RoleA unique service role predefined by AWS and directly linked to a specific service. Contains all permissions that service needs to call other AWS services.
sections.contentVi: IAM role tương tự IAM user nhưng có những khác biệt quan trọng: Không có long-term credentials — không có password, không có permanent access keys, chỉ cung cấp temporary credentials cho thời gian của session được assume. Nhiều principal có thể assume — không giống IAM user (gắn với một người), role có thể được assume bởi EC2 instance, Lambda function, ECS task và nhiều dịch vụ khác đồng thời. Các loại IAM role: Cross-Account Role — cấp quyền truy cập tài nguyên một tài khoản cho principal tin cậy trong tài khoản AWS khác, phương pháp chính cho kiến trúc multi-account. AWS Service Role — role mà AWS service (như EC2, Lambda) assume để thực hiện hành động trong tài khoản CỦA BẠN, chỉ trong phạm vi tài khoản của bạn. AWS Service-Linked Role — service role đặc biệt được AWS định nghĩa sẵn, gắn trực tiếp với một dịch vụ cụ thể, chứa tất cả quyền dịch vụ đó cần.

sections.label: Exam Focus
sections.heading: Role vs User: Key Distinctions
sections.contentHtml:
• IAM user = one person, long-term credentials (password + access keys), permanent identity.
  • IAM role = assumed by services/users/external identities, temporary credentials, no password.
  • Cross-account access → create a cross-account IAM role in the target account and allow the source account's principal to assume it.
  • Federated access (SAML 2.0, OIDC, Cognito) → external users assume an IAM role. They never get IAM user credentials.
  • EC2 instance needing AWS service access → always use an IAM role via instance profile, never long-term access keys.
Remember: An IAM role is assumed — when a service assumes a role, it gets temporary credentials via AWS STS (Security Token Service). The role itself has no static credentials.
sections.contentVi: IAM user = một người, long-term credentials (password + access keys), identity cố định. IAM role = được assume bởi services/users/external identities, temporary credentials, không có password. Cross-account access → tạo cross-account IAM role trong target account và cho phép principal của source account assume nó. Federated access (SAML 2.0, OIDC, Cognito) → users bên ngoài assume IAM role, không bao giờ nhận IAM user credentials. EC2 instance cần truy cập AWS service → luôn dùng IAM role qua instance profile, không bao giờ dùng long-term access keys. Nhớ: IAM role được assume — khi service assume role, nó nhận temporary credentials qua AWS STS (Security Token Service). Bản thân role không có static credentials.

summary: IAM User: represents a person or service, has long-term credentials (password + access key pair), tied to one identity
IAM Group: collection of users for simplified permission management; groups cannot be nested; no default group; groups have no credentials
IAM Role: assumed by services, accounts, or federated users; provides temporary credentials via STS; no password or permanent access keys
Cross-account role: the primary mechanism for granting access across AWS accounts; the target account creates the role and trusts the source account
Service-linked roles are predefined by AWS and contain all permissions a specific service needs to operate on your behalf
summaryVi: IAM User: đại diện người hoặc service, có long-term credentials (password + access key pair), gắn với một identity
IAM Group: tập hợp users để quản lý quyền dễ hơn; groups không thể lồng nhau; không có default group; groups không có credentials
IAM Role: được assume bởi services, accounts hoặc federated users; cung cấp temporary credentials qua STS; không có password hay permanent access keys
Cross-account role: cơ chế chính để cấp quyền truy cập giữa các AWS accounts; target account tạo role và tin tưởng source account
Service-linked roles được AWS định nghĩa sẵn và chứa tất cả quyền một dịch vụ cụ thể cần để hoạt động thay mặt bạn

quiz.question: A company has 50 developers who all need the same set of AWS permissions. A new developer joins and needs the same access. An existing developer leaves and their access must be revoked. Which IAM approach provides the LEAST operational overhead for ongoing access management?
options: Create an individual IAM policy for each developer and update it when permissions change
Add all developers to an IAM group with the required policy attached to the group
Create one IAM user with the required permissions and share its credentials with all developers
Create an IAM role and have each developer assume it using their personal computers
quiz.explanation: An IAM group with the required policy allows you to add new developers by adding them to the group and revoke access by removing them — the policy itself never changes. Individual policies per developer require editing each user's permissions separately. Sharing a single IAM user's credentials means you cannot revoke one person's access without changing the shared password for everyone. IAM roles are for temporary assumption by services, not for persistent developer access.

quiz.question: An organization has an IAM group structure where they want to create a sub-group of senior developers within a larger developers group. The senior developers group would inherit permissions from the developers group plus additional admin rights. Is this possible with IAM groups?
options: Yes — IAM groups can be nested up to three levels deep
Yes — but only if the parent group is an AWS managed group
No — IAM groups cannot contain other IAM groups; they can only contain IAM users
No — but you can use IAM roles as a substitute for nested groups
quiz.explanation: IAM groups cannot be nested — they can only contain IAM users, not other IAM groups. To achieve the desired setup, you would create two separate groups (developers and senior-developers), each with their own policies, and add senior developers to both groups. An IAM user can belong to multiple groups and will inherit the combined permissions of all groups they belong to.

quiz.question: A Lambda function needs to write to a DynamoDB table and send messages to an SQS queue. The Lambda function should have the minimum required permissions. Which IAM construct enables this without storing long-term credentials?
options: Create an IAM user with DynamoDB and SQS permissions and embed its access keys in the Lambda environment variables
Create an IAM group for Lambda functions with DynamoDB and SQS permissions
Create an IAM role with DynamoDB and SQS permissions and assign it as the Lambda execution role
Use the root user's credentials stored in Lambda's environment variables
quiz.explanation: An IAM role assigned as a Lambda execution role provides temporary credentials that Lambda automatically retrieves and rotates — no long-term credentials are stored. Lambda assumes the role during execution and STS provides temporary credentials. Embedding IAM user access keys in environment variables is insecure (they could be exposed in logs or code). Groups are for organizing users, not for Lambda functions. Root credentials should never be used in applications.

quiz.question: Company A (AWS Account A) needs to give Company B (AWS Account B) read access to Company A's S3 analytics bucket. Company B's employees should NOT receive IAM user accounts in Account A. Which is the MOST secure approach?
options: Create IAM users in Account A for each of Company B's employees and share the credentials
Make the S3 bucket public so Company B can access it without credentials
Create a cross-account IAM role in Account A that trusts Account B; Account B's users assume the role
Give Company B the Account A root credentials for temporary use
quiz.explanation: A cross-account IAM role in Account A trusts Account B — Company B's authorized users assume the role using their own credentials (sts:AssumeRole) to receive temporary credentials for Account A's S3 bucket. No permanent credentials are shared. Creating IAM users for external parties is operationally complex and risky. Making a bucket public removes all access control. Sharing root credentials is a critical security violation.

quiz.question: An EC2 Auto Scaling group launches new instances to handle traffic spikes. Each instance needs to read from S3 and write to CloudWatch Logs. How should the architect configure IAM access for these dynamically launched instances?
options: Manually create IAM users for each instance after it launches and attach access keys
Embed access keys in the AMI used by the Auto Scaling group
Attach an IAM role to the launch template — all new instances inherit the role via instance profile
Use the same IAM group policy for all EC2 instances
quiz.explanation: An IAM role specified in the Auto Scaling launch template is automatically applied as an instance profile to every new EC2 instance. All instances receive temporary, auto-rotated credentials without any manual configuration. Manually creating IAM users per instance doesn't scale. Embedding access keys in an AMI is a critical security risk — the keys are visible to anyone who accesses the AMI. IAM groups contain users, not EC2 instances.

quiz.question: An AWS service called AWS Config needs to evaluate compliance rules in a company's account. The architect notices there is a pre-created IAM role named 'AWSServiceRoleForConfig' that AWS Config uses. This role was created automatically when Config was enabled. What type of IAM role is this?
options: Cross-account IAM role
Customer-created AWS service role
AWS service-linked role
IAM identity-based role
quiz.explanation: An AWS service-linked role is a unique type of service role directly linked to a specific AWS service (like AWS Config). It is predefined by the service, automatically created when the service is enabled, and contains all the permissions that service needs to call other AWS services on the customer's behalf. Cross-account roles are for multi-account access. Customer-created service roles are manually created by customers. 'IAM identity-based role' is not a specific IAM role type.

quiz.question: A company uses SAML 2.0 federation — employees authenticate via their corporate Active Directory and access AWS resources. When an employee logs in, they are assigned AWS permissions. Which IAM mechanism enables this federated access?
options: The employee's Active Directory username is automatically mapped to an IAM user in AWS
Federated employees assume an IAM role configured with a SAML identity provider trust — they receive temporary AWS credentials
Federated employees receive permanent IAM access keys through the SAML token
Active Directory directly authenticates to AWS without IAM using a special AD gateway
quiz.explanation: In SAML 2.0 federation, the corporate identity provider (Active Directory) authenticates the user and sends a SAML assertion to AWS STS. AWS STS validates the assertion and provides temporary credentials for an IAM role configured to trust the SAML provider. The employee never has an IAM user — they assume a role. No permanent credentials are issued through federation.

quiz.question: A developer creates a new IAM user for a junior team member. By default, what level of AWS access does the new IAM user have before any policies are attached?
options: Full read-only access to all AWS services by default
The same access as the IAM group they were automatically added to
No permissions — a new IAM user has no access until policies are explicitly attached
Access to the AWS Management Console only, with no API access
quiz.explanation: In IAM, all requests are implicitly denied by default. A new IAM user has zero permissions until at least one policy allowing specific actions is attached. There is no default group in IAM that automatically includes new users. No implicit permissions are granted — everything requires explicit allow statements. This is part of the IAM evaluation logic: start with implicit deny, then explicit allows override it.

quiz.question: A solutions architect needs to choose the correct IAM construct for two scenarios. Scenario 1: An EC2 instance in Account A needs to call DynamoDB in Account B. Scenario 2: An application on EC2 needs to read from S3 in the same account. Which TWO IAM constructs correctly solve each scenario? (Choose TWO)
options: Cross-account IAM role in Account B, trusted by Account A's EC2 role, for Scenario 1
IAM user access keys stored on the EC2 instance for Scenario 1
IAM role attached via instance profile for Scenario 2
IAM group with S3 read policy for Scenario 2
Root user credentials shared with the EC2 instance for both scenarios
quiz.explanation: Scenario 1 (cross-account): a cross-account IAM role in Account B that trusts Account A's EC2 instance role — the EC2 instance assumes the cross-account role via STS to access DynamoDB in Account B. Scenario 2 (same-account): an IAM role via instance profile provides temporary credentials without storing long-term keys. IAM user access keys on EC2 are insecure. IAM groups contain users, not EC2 instances. Root credentials should never be used for application access.

quiz.question: A startup wants all developers to have access to CloudWatch Logs for debugging. A new developer named Alice is hired. The team lead adds Alice to the 'Developers' IAM group without attaching any additional policies to Alice's IAM user directly. Which permissions does Alice have?
options: No permissions — individual policies must be attached directly to Alice's user
All permissions of all IAM groups in the account
The combined permissions of all IAM groups Alice belongs to, including the Developers group
Only permissions explicitly attached to Alice's IAM user — group policies don't apply
quiz.explanation: An IAM user inherits the policies of all IAM groups they belong to. Alice receives all permissions attached to the Developers group (including CloudWatch Logs access). If Alice is later added to additional groups, her permissions expand to include those groups' policies too. IAM users do not need individual policies — group membership is sufficient. An IAM user does not inherit permissions from groups they are NOT a member of.

quiz.question: A company's security policy states that temporary credentials should be used for all programmatic access to AWS. Which IAM identity type provides temporary security credentials that automatically expire after a configurable duration?
options: IAM user with MFA enabled
IAM user with access key rotation every 90 days
IAM role assumed via AWS STS
IAM group with time-based access policies
quiz.explanation: When an IAM role is assumed via AWS Security Token Service (STS), it issues temporary security credentials (access key ID, secret access key, and session token) that expire automatically after a configurable duration (15 minutes to 36 hours). IAM users with access keys have long-term credentials that don't expire unless manually rotated or deleted. MFA adds an authentication factor but doesn't make access keys temporary. IAM groups don't have their own credentials.

quiz.question: An IAM administrator is reviewing their account and sees an IAM role with a trust policy referencing 'ec2.amazonaws.com' as the trusted principal. What does this trust relationship indicate?
options: Only EC2 instances in us-east-1 can assume this role
EC2 service can assume this role to perform actions on behalf of the account
This role can only access EC2-related AWS services
An EC2 instance in another account can assume this role
quiz.explanation: A trust policy with 'ec2.amazonaws.com' as the trusted principal means the Amazon EC2 service is allowed to assume this role on behalf of your account. This is an AWS service role — EC2 instances use this role (via instance profile) to receive temporary credentials. The role's permissions determine what EC2 can do, not the trust policy. The trust policy only controls who/what can assume the role, not what it can access.

quiz.question: A company wants to allow mobile app users to access their personal S3 objects. Users authenticate with Google (OIDC). After authentication, each user should only access their own objects in S3. Which IAM construct enables this?
options: Create an IAM user for each mobile app user
Configure a web identity IAM role with a trust for Google's OIDC provider; use policy variables to scope S3 access to the user's folder
Give all mobile users a shared IAM group with S3 full access
Use the root account for mobile user authentication
quiz.explanation: A web identity IAM role with a trust for Google's OIDC provider allows Google-authenticated users to assume the role and receive temporary AWS credentials. IAM policy variables (like ${aws:userid}) can scope S3 access to user-specific prefixes, ensuring each user only accesses their own objects. Creating IAM users for each mobile app user doesn't scale (could be millions of users). A shared group gives all users access to all objects. Root account should never be used for application authentication.

quiz.question: An architect needs to implement a solution where Account A's EC2 instances can read from Account B's S3 bucket. Account B must retain full control over which principals can access its bucket. Which combination implements this correctly?
options: Create IAM users in Account B and share credentials with Account A's EC2 instances
Create an IAM role in Account A that trusts Account B and grants S3 read permissions in Account A
Create a cross-account IAM role in Account B that trusts Account A; add an S3 bucket policy in Account B granting the cross-account role read access
Enable VPC peering between Account A and Account B's VPCs
quiz.explanation: The correct cross-account pattern: (1) create a role in Account B that trusts Account A; (2) add an S3 bucket policy in Account B explicitly allowing the cross-account role — this ensures Account B retains control. Account A's EC2 instances assume the Account B role via STS. Creating users in Account B and sharing credentials is insecure and hard to manage. A role in Account A cannot grant access to Account B's S3. VPC peering enables network connectivity, not IAM authorization for S3.

quiz.question: An IAM user leaves the company. The administrator deletes the user from IAM. The user had one inline policy (DynamoDB access) and was a member of an IAM group (S3ReadOnly policy). What is the effect of deleting the user?
options: The inline DynamoDB policy is automatically deleted; the S3ReadOnly group policy remains attached to the group
Both the inline policy and the S3ReadOnly group policy are permanently deleted from IAM
The inline policy is moved to a policy archive; the group policy is unaffected
The inline policy remains in IAM unattached; the group policy is transferred to another user
quiz.explanation: Inline policies are tightly coupled to their identity — when the IAM user is deleted, the inline DynamoDB policy is automatically deleted with it. However, the S3ReadOnly policy is a managed policy attached to an IAM group — it remains intact and attached to the group. Deleting a user from the group simply removes their membership; it doesn't affect the group's policy or other members. Inline policies cannot exist independently and have no archive state.

lessons.title: IAM Policy Types
lessons.overview: Understand the full spectrum of IAM policy types — identity-based, resource-based, permissions boundaries, SCPs, S3 ACLs, and session policies — and know when each type applies, what it can and cannot do.
lessons.overviewVi: Hiểu toàn bộ các loại IAM policy — identity-based, resource-based, permissions boundaries, SCPs, S3 ACLs và session policies — và biết khi nào mỗi loại được áp dụng, có thể và không thể làm gì.

sections.label: Overview
sections.heading: Six IAM Policy Types
sections.contentHtml:
AWS evaluates multiple policy types when determining whether to allow or deny a request. Understanding each type is critical for the SAA-C03 exam:

Identity-basedAttached to IAM user, group, or role — controls what the identity CAN DO
Resource-basedAttached to a resource (S3, SQS, Lambda) — controls WHO CAN ACCESS the resource; enables cross-account access
Permissions boundarySets the MAXIMUM permissions an identity-based policy can grant — does NOT grant permissions itself
SCP (Service Control Policy)AWS Organizations — sets maximum permissions for accounts/OUs — does NOT grant permissions; cannot affect root user
S3 ACLCross-account access control for S3 buckets at the object/bucket level — uses a predefined grant structure, not JSON
Session policyPassed during role assumption via STS — limits the session's permissions without granting new ones
sections.contentVi: AWS đánh giá nhiều loại policy khi xác định có cho phép hay từ chối một request. Sáu loại policy: Identity-based — gắn vào IAM user, group hoặc role, kiểm soát identity đó CÓ THỂ LÀM GÌ. Resource-based — gắn vào tài nguyên (S3, SQS, Lambda), kiểm soát AI CÓ THỂ TRUY CẬP tài nguyên, kích hoạt cross-account access. Permissions boundary — đặt giới hạn TỐI ĐA quyền identity-based policy có thể cấp, KHÔNG tự cấp quyền. SCP (Service Control Policy) — AWS Organizations, đặt quyền tối đa cho accounts/OUs, KHÔNG cấp quyền, không ảnh hưởng root user. S3 ACL — kiểm soát cross-account access cho S3 buckets, dùng cấu trúc predefined grant, không phải JSON. Session policy — được truyền khi assume role qua STS, giới hạn quyền của session mà không cấp thêm quyền.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: Identity-Based and Resource-Based Policies
sections.contentHtml:
Identity-based policies come in three forms:

  • AWS Managed — created by AWS, aligned to job functions (e.g. AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess). You can attach but not modify.
  • Customer Managed — created and fully controlled by you. Can be attached to multiple identities.
  • Inline — embedded in one specific identity. One-to-one relationship. Auto-deleted when the identity is deleted.

Resource-based policies are always inline (no managed version). Key examples:

S3 bucket policyGrants IAM users or other accounts access to a specific bucket and its objects
SQS access policyGrants external accounts or companies permission to send/receive messages on a queue
IAM role trust policyDeclares which entities (principals) are trusted to assume the role and under what conditions
Key difference: Identity-based policies include the resource in their statements. Resource-based policies include the principal (who can access) in their statements.
sections.contentVi: Identity-based policies có ba dạng: AWS Managed — được AWS tạo, theo job functions (VD: AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess), gắn được nhưng không chỉnh sửa được. Customer Managed — do bạn tạo và kiểm soát hoàn toàn, gắn được vào nhiều identity. Inline — nhúng trong một identity cụ thể, quan hệ một-một, tự xóa khi identity bị xóa. Resource-based policies luôn là inline (không có managed version). Ví dụ chính: S3 bucket policy — cấp quyền cho IAM users hoặc accounts khác truy cập bucket và objects. SQS access policy — cấp quyền cho accounts bên ngoài gửi/nhận messages trên queue. IAM role trust policy — khai báo entities nào (principals) được tin tưởng để assume role và điều kiện gì. Khác biệt chính: identity-based policies ghi resource trong statements; resource-based policies ghi principal (ai được truy cập) trong statements.

sections.label: Deep Dive
sections.heading: Permissions Boundaries, SCPs, and Session Policies
sections.contentHtml:
These three policy types are guardrails that limit permissions — they do NOT grant permissions on their own:

Permissions BoundaryApplied to individual IAM users/roles. An action is only allowed if BOTH the identity-based policy AND the boundary allow it. Prevents over-provisioning by delegated admins.
Service Control Policy (SCP)Applied at AWS Organization account or OU level. Limits what IAM policies in that account can grant. Does NOT grant permissions. Cannot restrict the root user of a member account.
Session PolicyPassed as a parameter when calling sts:AssumeRole. Limits the session's permissions below what the role allows — useful for granting a subset of a role's permissions for a specific task.

S3 ACLs are cross-account permission grants. Unlike bucket policies (which use JSON), ACLs use predefined grants (e.g. bucket-owner-full-control, READ, WRITE). ACLs cannot grant permissions to entities in the same account.

SCP critical rule: SCPs cannot grant permissions — they can only restrict. Even if an IAM policy in an account allows an action, an SCP denying it will block it.
sections.contentVi: Ba loại policy này là guardrails giới hạn quyền — KHÔNG tự cấp quyền: Permissions Boundary — áp dụng cho individual IAM users/roles; một hành động chỉ được phép nếu CẢ identity-based policy VÀ boundary đều cho phép; ngăn over-provisioning bởi delegated admins. Service Control Policy (SCP) — áp dụng ở cấp AWS Organization account hoặc OU; giới hạn những gì IAM policies trong tài khoản đó có thể cấp; KHÔNG cấp quyền; không thể hạn chế root user của member account. Session Policy — truyền khi gọi sts:AssumeRole; giới hạn quyền của session xuống dưới mức role cho phép, hữu ích khi cần cấp subset quyền của role cho một task cụ thể. S3 ACLs là cross-account permission grants. Không giống bucket policies (JSON), ACLs dùng predefined grants (bucket-owner-full-control, READ, WRITE). ACLs KHÔNG thể cấp quyền cho entities trong cùng account.

sections.label: Exam Focus
sections.heading: Policy Type Decision Tree
sections.contentHtml:
• "Grant permissions to an IAM user/role" → identity-based policy (managed or inline)
  • "Allow cross-account access to S3/SQS/Lambda" → resource-based policy on the resource
  • "Prevent a delegated admin from giving themselves more permissions than they have" → permissions boundary
  • "Restrict all accounts in an AWS Organization from using a specific service" → SCP attached to the OU or root
  • "Restrict temporary credentials to a subset of role permissions" → session policy via sts:AssumeRole
  • SCP vs Permissions Boundary: SCP = org-level, applies to an entire account; Permissions Boundary = identity-level, applies to one user or role
Remember: Both SCPs and Permissions Boundaries can only RESTRICT — they cannot grant. An explicit Allow in an SCP is meaningless without a corresponding Allow in the identity-based policy. An entity can only do what ALL applicable policies allow simultaneously.
sections.contentVi: Cây quyết định loại policy: 'Cấp quyền cho IAM user/role' → identity-based policy (managed hoặc inline). 'Cho phép cross-account access đến S3/SQS/Lambda' → resource-based policy trên tài nguyên. 'Ngăn delegated admin tự cấp quyền cao hơn quyền họ đang có' → permissions boundary. 'Hạn chế tất cả accounts trong AWS Organization dùng một dịch vụ cụ thể' → SCP gắn vào OU hoặc root. 'Giới hạn temporary credentials xuống subset quyền của role' → session policy qua sts:AssumeRole. SCP vs Permissions Boundary: SCP = cấp tổ chức, áp dụng cho cả account; Permissions Boundary = cấp identity, áp dụng cho một user hoặc role. Nhớ: Cả SCP và Permissions Boundary chỉ có thể GIỚI HẠN — không thể cấp. Một entity chỉ làm được những gì TẤT CẢ policies áp dụng đều cho phép cùng lúc.

summary: Identity-based policies (managed/inline) control what an IAM identity can do; resource-based policies control who can access a specific resource
Permissions boundaries and SCPs are guardrail policies — they can only restrict, never grant permissions on their own
SCP applies at the AWS Organization account/OU level; permissions boundary applies to individual IAM users or roles
S3 ACLs use predefined grants (not JSON), work cross-account, and cannot grant permissions to entities within the same account
Session policies are passed at sts:AssumeRole time to limit a specific session's permissions to a subset of the role's allowed actions
summaryVi: Identity-based policies kiểm soát identity có thể làm gì; resource-based policies kiểm soát ai có thể truy cập tài nguyên cụ thể
Permissions boundaries và SCPs là guardrail policies — chỉ giới hạn, không tự cấp quyền
SCP áp dụng ở cấp AWS Organization account/OU; permissions boundary áp dụng cho individual IAM users hoặc roles
S3 ACLs dùng predefined grants (không phải JSON), hoạt động cross-account, không thể cấp quyền cho entities trong cùng account
Session policies được truyền khi sts:AssumeRole để giới hạn quyền của session cụ thể xuống subset của role

quiz.question: A company uses AWS Organizations with multiple member accounts. The security team wants to prevent all accounts in the Production OU from creating IAM users with administrator access. IAM administrators in each account should still be able to manage their own users. Which policy type enforces this organization-wide restriction?
options: Permissions boundary attached to every IAM user in every account
Resource-based policy on each account's IAM service
Service Control Policy (SCP) attached to the Production OU
An inline deny policy in each account's administrator IAM group
quiz.explanation: A Service Control Policy (SCP) attached to the Production OU restricts all accounts within the OU from performing the specified actions — even if an account's IAM administrator creates a policy allowing it, the SCP prevents it. SCPs apply at the organization/OU/account level without requiring changes in each individual account. Permissions boundaries require attaching to each individual user — not scalable. Resource-based policies on IAM don't exist. Inline deny policies in each account require manual maintenance in every account.

quiz.question: A company allows junior administrators to create IAM roles for application teams. The security team is concerned that a junior admin could create a role with more permissions than they themselves have. Which IAM feature prevents junior admins from escalating privileges when creating roles?
options: Service Control Policy that denies IAM role creation
Permissions boundary — require junior admins to attach a boundary when creating roles, limiting the roles' maximum permissions
An S3 bucket policy that restricts IAM console access
An SCP that prevents the junior admin's account from accessing IAM
quiz.explanation: Permissions boundaries are specifically designed to prevent privilege escalation by delegated administrators. By requiring junior admins to attach a permissions boundary when creating roles, you ensure the created roles can never have more permissions than the boundary allows — even if the junior admin writes a policy granting AdministratorAccess. The boundary caps the effective permissions. SCPs work at the account level, not at the individual-admin level. S3 bucket policies are for S3 access, not IAM console control.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is designing an S3 bucket policy for a vendor who needs access. The vendor's AWS account ID is known. The policy grants the vendor's account permission to GetObject on the bucket. Which policy type is being configured on the S3 bucket?
options: Identity-based policy attached to the vendor's IAM user
Permissions boundary attached to the S3 bucket
Resource-based policy (S3 bucket policy) that specifies the vendor account as the principal
Service Control Policy attached to the S3 bucket's OU
quiz.explanation: An S3 bucket policy is a resource-based policy attached to the S3 bucket that specifies the principal (vendor account ID) and the allowed actions. This enables cross-account access without creating IAM users for the vendor. Identity-based policies are attached to IAM identities in your own account, not to external vendors directly. Permissions boundaries apply to IAM identities, not S3 buckets. SCPs apply to AWS Organization accounts, not individual S3 buckets.

quiz.question: An organization's SCP for a member account explicitly allows S3:GetObject. The member account's IAM policy also explicitly allows S3:GetObject. However, a user in the account still cannot perform S3:GetObject. What is the MOST likely explanation?
options: SCPs grant permissions, so the SCP Allow should be sufficient — the IAM policy may have a conflicting deny

SCPs cannot grant permissions — the SCP Allow only removes the SCP restriction, but the permission must come from the IAM policy; both are needed and present, so there may be an explicit deny from a third policy type
S3:GetObject requires a resource-based policy on the bucket regardless of SCP and IAM policies
The root user's permissions are blocking the request
quiz.explanation: SCPs cannot grant permissions on their own — they only define the maximum boundary. For an action to be allowed, it must be explicitly allowed by both the SCP (no restriction) and the IAM policy. In this scenario, both allow the action, but there could be an explicit deny from another policy type (like a permissions boundary, session policy, or an explicit deny in another statement). An explicit deny anywhere in the evaluation chain always overrides allows.

quiz.question: A DevOps engineer needs temporary access to assume a role for a specific deployment task. They should only be able to perform S3 PutObject during this session — not all actions the role normally allows. Which mechanism limits the session to just S3 PutObject?
options: Create a new IAM role with only S3 PutObject permissions for the engineer
Attach a permissions boundary to the role before the engineer assumes it
Pass a session policy when calling sts:AssumeRole that limits permissions to S3 PutObject
Apply an SCP to the account that restricts all actions except S3 PutObject
quiz.explanation: A session policy passed to sts:AssumeRole limits the session's effective permissions to the intersection of the role's permissions and the session policy. The engineer assumes the role normally but their session is scoped to only S3 PutObject for that specific task. Creating a new role adds operational overhead. Permissions boundaries are attached to identities and require prior setup. An SCP restricts the entire account — not a single session for one engineer.

quiz.question: A company needs to share objects in an S3 bucket with a partner company in a different AWS account. The partner should have read access to individual objects. The team wants to use S3 ACLs rather than bucket policies. Which statement about S3 ACLs is accurate?
options: S3 ACLs use JSON format and support the same conditions as bucket policies
S3 ACLs can grant permissions to entities within the same AWS account using predefined grants
S3 ACLs are cross-account permission grants using predefined grants (e.g. READ, WRITE) — they cannot grant permissions to entities in the same account
S3 ACLs are applied at the IAM user level, not the S3 bucket/object level
quiz.explanation: S3 ACLs grant cross-account permissions using predefined grants (READ, WRITE, FULL_CONTROL, READ_ACP, WRITE_ACP) rather than a JSON policy document. A key constraint is that ACLs cannot grant permissions to principals within the same AWS account — that requires bucket policies or identity-based policies. ACLs are applied at the bucket or object level, not the IAM identity level.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is reviewing IAM policy evaluation for a user in an AWS Organization member account. The user has an identity-based policy, the account has an SCP, and the user has a permissions boundary. Which TWO statements correctly describe when an action is allowed? (Choose TWO)
options: The action must be explicitly allowed by the identity-based policy
The SCP must explicitly allow the action (not just fail to deny it)
The permissions boundary must not deny the action — the action must be within the boundary's allowed set
The root user in the same account can override any SCP restriction
An explicit deny in any policy type overrides all allow statements
quiz.explanation: For an action to succeed when all three policy types are present: (1) the identity-based policy must explicitly allow it (Option A); and (2) an explicit deny in ANY policy overrides all allows (Option E — fundamental IAM rule). SCPs don't need to explicitly allow every action — they just cannot explicitly deny it; the absence of a deny in an SCP is sufficient (Option B is wrong). Permissions boundaries need to not deny the action, but they also don't need to explicitly allow every action — just not deny (Option C is partially correct but not the precise rule; the precise rule is that the effective permissions are the intersection of identity policy and boundary). The root user of a member account IS affected by SCPs — Option D is wrong.

quiz.question: An architect wants to give a Lambda function the ability to write to specific CloudWatch Logs log groups. The function should not be able to write to any other log group, even if future policies are attached that grant broader CloudWatch access. Which approach enforces this persistent restriction?
options: Attach an IAM managed policy with CloudWatch full access, then periodically review CloudWatch usage
Attach an identity-based policy with specific CloudWatch Logs PutLogEvents permission scoped to the specific log group ARNs
Attach a permissions boundary to the Lambda execution role that limits CloudWatch Logs to the specific log group ARNs
Add an SCP to restrict CloudWatch access for the entire account to only those log groups
quiz.explanation: A permissions boundary attached to the Lambda execution role ensures that no matter what identity-based policies are attached now or in the future, the role's effective permissions can never exceed the boundary. Even if an over-permissive policy is added later, the boundary limits access to the specified log group ARNs. A standalone identity-based policy can always be replaced or supplemented by a broader policy. An SCP restricts the entire account — not just one Lambda role.

quiz.question: A company has a policy that inline policies should not be used in their AWS environment, only managed policies. What is the MAIN disadvantage of inline policies that justifies this rule?
options: Inline policies cannot contain deny statements
Inline policies are limited to 5 KB in size
Inline policies cannot be reused across multiple identities and are deleted with the identity — leading to policy duplication and harder auditing
Inline policies are always more permissive than managed policies
quiz.explanation: Inline policies have a strict one-to-one relationship with the identity they're embedded in — they cannot be attached to other users/groups/roles and are automatically deleted when the identity is deleted. This makes it harder to maintain consistent permissions across multiple entities and complicates auditing. Managed policies are standalone objects that can be attached to multiple identities, centrally updated, and independently versioned. Inline policies support deny statements and have no special size limitation that makes them problematic.

quiz.question: A multi-account organization has an SCP at the root organizational unit (OU) that denies all EC2 actions. A team in a member account tries to launch an EC2 instance using a role with AdministratorAccess. What happens?
options: The EC2 launch succeeds because AdministratorAccess overrides SCPs
The EC2 launch fails — the SCP deny overrides the identity-based AdministratorAccess policy
The EC2 launch succeeds because SCPs only affect non-administrator roles
The SCP is ignored because it is applied at the root OU level, not the member account level
quiz.explanation: SCPs take precedence — even AdministratorAccess cannot override an SCP deny. SCPs applied at the root OU propagate to all member accounts and OUs beneath it. An explicit deny in an SCP effectively makes that action impossible for all IAM identities in affected accounts, regardless of their identity-based policies. SCPs do not exempt administrator-level roles. SCPs at the root OU do apply to all member accounts.

quiz.question: A team needs to grant a third-party auditing tool (in an external AWS account) read-only access to AWS CloudTrail logs stored in an S3 bucket. The bucket owner wants to maintain control. Which approach requires changes in ONLY the bucket owner's account?
options: Create an IAM user in the bucket owner's account and share credentials with the auditing tool
Add an S3 bucket policy in the bucket owner's account granting the auditing tool's AWS account read access
Set up a VPN connection and use network-level access control
Enable S3 public access on the CloudTrail logs bucket
quiz.explanation: An S3 bucket policy (resource-based policy) in the bucket owner's account grants the external auditing tool's AWS account read access using their account ID or role ARN. Changes are made only in the bucket owner's account — no IAM changes are needed in the auditing tool's account (they use their own credentials to access the bucket). Creating IAM users requires changes in the owner's account and credential management. VPN doesn't control S3 access. Making the bucket public removes all access control.

quiz.question: An administrator is configuring IAM for a multi-account environment. They want to ensure that even if an account administrator attaches a policy allowing an IAM user to create EC2 instances, the user cannot actually create EC2 instances. The control should be applied at the organizational level, not per account. Which IAM construct implements this?
options: Permissions boundary on every IAM user in the account
An inline deny policy embedded in every IAM user
An SCP attached to the account or OU that explicitly denies EC2:RunInstances
An IAM role trust policy that blocks EC2 access
quiz.explanation: An SCP denying EC2:RunInstances applied at the OU or account level prevents that action for all IAM identities in the account — even if account administrators explicitly allow it in IAM policies. This is org-level enforcement without requiring changes in individual accounts. Permissions boundaries require attaching to every user individually. Inline deny policies require manual maintenance in every account. A role trust policy controls who can assume a role, not what actions can be performed.

quiz.question: A developer assumes an IAM role with broad S3 and DynamoDB permissions for a CI/CD pipeline job. For this specific job, they should only write to one S3 bucket prefix. The permissions should return to normal after the job. Which is the MOST targeted approach?
options: Modify the IAM role's policy to restrict to the specific S3 prefix before the job and revert after
Create a new IAM role specifically for this job with only the S3 prefix permissions
Pass a session policy when calling sts:AssumeRole that scopes access to the specific S3 bucket and prefix
Use a permissions boundary that limits S3 to the specific prefix
quiz.explanation: A session policy passed at assume-role time scopes the session's effective permissions to the intersection of the role's permissions and the session policy — without modifying the role itself. After the job, normal role assumptions are unaffected. Modifying the role policy is risky (other sessions using the role are affected during the change) and requires reversion. Creating a new role adds operational overhead and doesn't automatically clean up. Permissions boundaries are set before role assumption and affect all future sessions.

quiz.question: A company's IAM policy for a role states: Statement 1 allows s3:GetObject on all buckets. Statement 2 explicitly denies s3:GetObject on a specific confidential bucket ARN. If a user assumes this role and tries to access the confidential bucket, what happens?
options: The allow in Statement 1 takes precedence because it was written first
The request succeeds because the user has a broad allow for all S3 buckets
The request is denied — an explicit deny always overrides an explicit allow, regardless of order
The result depends on whether the bucket policy also allows the access
quiz.explanation: In IAM, explicit deny always overrides explicit allow — regardless of statement order or the number of allow statements. Even though Statement 1 broadly allows s3:GetObject on all buckets, Statement 2's explicit deny on the confidential bucket takes precedence. The evaluation starts with implicit deny, allows override it, but an explicit deny always wins. This is a fundamental rule of IAM policy evaluation.

quiz.question: A security team is implementing IAM controls for a new AWS Organization. Which TWO characteristics are TRUE about Service Control Policies (SCPs)? (Choose TWO)
options: An SCP that explicitly denies an action prevents all IAM identities in the affected account from performing that action, even those with AdministratorAccess
SCPs can grant permissions to IAM users who do not have any identity-based policies
SCPs do not affect the AWS account root user of a member account
SCPs apply to IAM users and roles in affected accounts but NOT to the management account
An SCP with Allow for an action is sufficient to allow that action without an identity-based policy
quiz.explanation: SCPs that deny an action block all IAM identities in the account — including those with AdministratorAccess (Option A is correct). SCPs applied at the OU level do not apply to the management account (also called the master/payer account) — they only apply to member accounts (Option D is correct). SCPs cannot grant permissions — they only restrict (Option B is wrong). The root user of a MEMBER account IS affected by SCPs — only the management account's root user is exempt (Option C is wrong). An SCP Allow alone is insufficient — an identity-based policy must also allow the action (Option E is wrong).

lessons.title: IAM Policy Basics
lessons.overview: Learn the anatomy of an IAM policy JSON document — the policy-wide elements (ID, Version) and the six statement elements (SID, Effect, Principal, Action, Resource, Condition) — with practical examples for MFA enforcement and cross-account S3 access.
lessons.overviewVi: Học cấu trúc của IAM policy JSON document — các phần tử chính sách (ID, Version) và sáu phần tử statement (SID, Effect, Principal, Action, Resource, Condition) — với ví dụ thực tế về MFA enforcement và cross-account S3 access.

sections.label: Overview
sections.heading: IAM Policy JSON Structure
sections.contentHtml:
Every IAM policy is a JSON document with two main components:

Policy-wide informationOptional elements at the top: ID (unique identifier) and Version (policy language version — usually "2012-10-17")
Statement(s)One or more individual permission statements. AWS applies a logical OR across statements. Each statement has up to 6 elements.
Note: The Version element refers to the AWS policy language version — not your policy version. It is almost always set to "2012-10-17". Using "2008-10-17" (older) disables policy variables like ${aws:username}.
sections.contentVi: Mỗi IAM policy là JSON document với hai thành phần chính: Policy-wide information — phần tử tùy chọn ở đầu: ID (định danh duy nhất) và Version (phiên bản ngôn ngữ policy — thường là '2012-10-17'). Statements — một hoặc nhiều permission statements riêng lẻ; AWS áp dụng logical OR giữa các statements; mỗi statement có đến 6 phần tử. Lưu ý: phần tử Version đề cập đến phiên bản ngôn ngữ AWS policy — không phải version policy của bạn. Hầu như luôn đặt là '2012-10-17'. Dùng '2008-10-17' (cũ hơn) sẽ vô hiệu hóa policy variables như ${aws:username}.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: The Six Statement Elements
sections.contentHtml:
SIDStatement ID — optional identifier to differentiate statements within a policy. Helps with readability and debugging.
EffectMandatory. Only two values: Allow or Deny. Explicitly states if the statement grants or blocks access.
PrincipalThe IAM identity the statement applies to (account, user, role, federated user). Required for resource-based policies. Omitted in identity-based policies (the attached identity IS the principal).
ActionList of API operations allowed/denied. Uses service:Action format. Wildcard * means ALL actions for that service (e.g. s3:*).
ResourceARN of the resource the action applies to. Use * for all resources. Required for identity-based policies. Optional for resource-based policies.
ConditionOptional. Conditional rules that must be met for the statement to apply. Uses condition operators (StringEquals, Bool, IpAddress, etc.) and condition keys.
sections.contentVi: Sáu phần tử statement: SID — Statement ID, tùy chọn, dùng để phân biệt các statements trong policy, giúp dễ đọc và debug. Effect — BẮT BUỘC, chỉ hai giá trị: Allow hoặc Deny, nêu rõ statement cấp hay chặn quyền. Principal — IAM identity mà statement áp dụng (account, user, role, federated user), BẮT BUỘC với resource-based policies, bỏ qua trong identity-based policies (identity được gắn chính là principal). Action — danh sách API operations được allow/deny, dùng format service:Action, wildcard * nghĩa là TẤT CẢ actions của service đó (VD: s3:*). Resource — ARN của tài nguyên mà action áp dụng, dùng * cho tất cả tài nguyên, BẮT BUỘC với identity-based policies, tùy chọn với resource-based. Condition — TÙY CHỌN, quy tắc điều kiện phải được đáp ứng để statement áp dụng, dùng condition operators (StringEquals, Bool, IpAddress,...) và condition keys.

sections.label: Deep Dive
sections.heading: Condition Operators and Practical Examples
sections.contentHtml:
Condition operators let you craft fine-grained, context-aware permissions:

StringEquals / StringLikeMatch string values exactly or with wildcards — useful for matching resource tags, usernames, prefixes
Bool / BoolIfExistsCheck boolean values — commonly used with aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent to enforce MFA
IpAddress / NotIpAddressMatch request source IP — restrict access to specific CIDR ranges
IfExists suffixAppended to any operator (e.g. BoolIfExists). If the condition key is absent from the request context, the condition evaluates to true.

Practical examples:

  • Deny S3 PutObject if MFA is not enabled: use BoolIfExists on aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent with value false
  • Allow cross-account S3 upload while ensuring bucket-owner gets full control: use StringEquals on s3:x-amz-acl with value bucket-owner-full-control
  • Scope resource access to a specific prefix: arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/* — the /* wildcard matches all objects inside the bucket
sections.contentVi: Condition operators cho phép tạo quyền có ngữ cảnh chi tiết: StringEquals / StringLike — khớp string chính xác hoặc với wildcard, dùng để khớp resource tags, usernames, prefixes. Bool / BoolIfExists — kiểm tra boolean values, thường dùng với aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent để enforce MFA. IpAddress / NotIpAddress — khớp IP nguồn của request, hạn chế truy cập theo CIDR ranges. IfExists suffix — thêm vào bất kỳ operator nào (VD: BoolIfExists); nếu condition key vắng mặt trong request context, condition đánh giá là true. Ví dụ thực tế: Từ chối S3 PutObject nếu MFA không được bật — dùng BoolIfExists trên aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent với giá trị false. Cho phép cross-account S3 upload nhưng bucket-owner vẫn có full control — dùng StringEquals trên s3:x-amz-acl với bucket-owner-full-control. Giới hạn resource access theo prefix cụ thể: arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*, wildcard /* khớp tất cả objects trong bucket.

sections.label: Exam Focus
sections.heading: Policy Element Rules to Remember
sections.contentHtml:
• Effect is the only mandatory statement element — everything else has constraints or is optional.
  • Principal: required in resource-based policies; forbidden/omitted in identity-based policies.
  • Action wildcards: s3:* = all S3 actions; * = all actions all services (used by AdministratorAccess).
  • Resource wildcards: * = all resources; arn:aws:s3:::bucket-name/* = all objects in a bucket (not the bucket itself).
  • IfExists operators: if the key is absent, condition evaluates to true — ensures the policy doesn't accidentally deny requests from services that don't send the key.
  • MFA condition pattern: use Deny + BoolIfExists:aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent: false to deny requests when MFA is absent.
Wildcard cheat sheet: * = any string; ? = any single character. Example: arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/home/${aws:username}/* — restricts each IAM user to their own S3 prefix.
sections.contentVi: Quy tắc policy element cần nhớ: Effect là phần tử statement DUY NHẤT BẮT BUỘC — tất cả phần tử khác có ràng buộc hoặc là tùy chọn.
