options: AWS Directory Service — centralize directory management across accounts
Amazon Cognito — replace SAML federation with Cognito Identity Pools
AWS IAM Identity Center — centralize SAML IdP configuration once; permissions applied centrally without per-account IAM provider setup
AWS Organizations SCPs — apply SAML restrictions at the organization level
quiz.explanation: AWS IAM Identity Center eliminates the need for per-account SAML identity provider configurations. You configure the SAML IdP once in Identity Center at the organization level. Identity Center then provisions IAM roles in each account based on permission sets and account assignments — without requiring individual SAML setup in each account. AWS Directory Service manages directories but doesn't centralize SAML federation. Cognito is for application-level identity management (mobile/web apps). SCPs are for restricting permissions, not managing federation.

quiz.question: A CFO asks the solutions architect whether AWS IAM Identity Center incurs additional charges on top of regular AWS service fees. What is the CORRECT answer?
options: Yes — IAM Identity Center charges $0.05 per active user per month
Yes — IAM Identity Center charges per SSO session initiated
No — AWS IAM Identity Center is provided at no additional service charge; you only pay for the AWS resources accessed
No — but it requires an AWS Enterprise Support plan which costs extra
quiz.explanation: AWS IAM Identity Center is provided at no additional cost. You do not pay for the Identity Center service itself — only for the AWS resources your users access (EC2 instances, S3 storage, etc.) through Identity Center. This is worth remembering for cost-based exam scenarios: when comparing solutions for multi-account SSO, Identity Center's zero additional cost is a clear advantage over building custom federation solutions.

quiz.question: A company uses AWS IAM Identity Center integrated with Azure Active Directory. A developer is assigned to the 'SandboxAccount' with the 'DeveloperAccess' permission set. The developer needs to run AWS CLI commands from their laptop. How do they obtain temporary AWS credentials for the CLI?
options: The IT team creates an IAM user in the SandboxAccount and emails the access keys to the developer
The developer logs into the AWS Access Portal and downloads temporary CLI credentials for the specific account and permission set
The developer uses their Azure AD password directly as the AWS secret access key
The developer must use the AWS Management Console only — CLI access is not supported with IAM Identity Center
quiz.explanation: In IAM Identity Center, developers access the AWS Access Portal where they see all their assigned accounts and permission sets. From the portal, they can: (1) open the AWS Management Console for an account, or (2) view and copy/download temporary CLI credentials (access key ID, secret access key, session token) for their assigned role. The AWS CLI v2 also natively supports the SSO login flow with 'aws sso login' and 'aws sso configure'. IAM Identity Center fully supports CLI access through temporary credentials — no permanent IAM users are needed.

quiz.question: A company has 50 AWS accounts. They want to give the Finance team read-only access to Cost Explorer in all accounts, while giving the DevOps team admin access to development accounts and read-only access to production accounts. What is the MOST efficient way to implement this using IAM Identity Center?
options: Create IAM users in every account for Finance and DevOps with appropriate policies
Create permission sets (CostExplorerReadOnly, AdminAccess, ReadOnly) and make account assignments mapping each group+permission set to the relevant accounts
Use AWS Organizations SCPs to grant Finance team Cost Explorer access
Configure cross-account IAM roles manually in each of the 50 accounts
quiz.explanation: IAM Identity Center's permission sets and account assignments are designed exactly for this use case. Create three permission sets: CostExplorerReadOnly, AdminAccess, ReadOnly. Assign: Finance group + CostExplorerReadOnly → all 50 accounts; DevOps group + AdminAccess → development accounts; DevOps group + ReadOnly → production accounts. Identity Center provisions the appropriate IAM roles in each account. This requires no IAM user management and no per-account configuration. SCPs can only restrict — they can't grant permissions to a specific team. Manual cross-account roles don't scale to 50 accounts.

quiz.question: A large enterprise is evaluating whether to use AWS IAM Identity Center or direct IAM SAML federation for their 200-account AWS environment. The security team cites complexity of per-account configuration as a concern with direct federation. Which advantage of IAM Identity Center directly addresses this concern?
options: IAM Identity Center provides cheaper SAML token validation than direct federation

IAM Identity Center centralizes the IdP configuration and account access management in one place — no need to configure SAML identity providers and trust policies in each of the 200 accounts individually
IAM Identity Center uses OIDC instead of SAML, which is more secure than direct SAML federation
IAM Identity Center automatically creates IAM users in each account, eliminating the need for roles
quiz.explanation: With direct IAM SAML federation, you must create and configure an IAM identity provider and IAM roles in each account separately — for 200 accounts, this is 200 configurations to maintain. IAM Identity Center centralizes all of this: configure the IdP once, define permission sets once, and manage account assignments from a single console. Identity Center provisions IAM roles in target accounts automatically. IAM Identity Center uses SAML and OIDC depending on the configuration — it doesn't replace SAML with OIDC exclusively. Identity Center provisions temporary roles, not IAM users.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is designing access for a startup that uses Google Workspace for their corporate identity. All employees should be able to log into their AWS accounts using their Google credentials. Which AWS service and configuration enables this?
options: Create IAM users manually for each Google Workspace user
Configure AWS IAM Identity Center with Google Workspace as an external SAML 2.0 identity provider — Google authenticates users and sends SAML assertions to Identity Center
Use Amazon Cognito User Pools to federate Google Workspace users into AWS
Set up AWS Single Sign-On (SSO) — a separate service from IAM Identity Center that supports Google Workspace specifically
quiz.explanation: Google Workspace supports SAML 2.0, so it can be configured as an external identity source in AWS IAM Identity Center. Employees authenticate with their Google credentials, Google sends a SAML assertion to Identity Center, and Identity Center provisions temporary credentials for the appropriate AWS account and permission set. No IAM users need to be created. Amazon Cognito is for application-level identity (mobile/web apps), not for centralizing multi-account AWS access. AWS IAM Identity Center is what was formerly called AWS SSO — they are the same service; there is no separate 'AWS SSO' service.

quiz.question: A company wants to allow their employees to access both their AWS accounts AND their Salesforce CRM using a single set of corporate credentials. They use AWS IAM Identity Center. Which capability supports access to Salesforce from Identity Center?
options: Identity Center can only manage access to AWS accounts — Salesforce requires a separate SSO provider
AWS IAM Identity Center supports SAML 2.0 integrations with business cloud applications like Salesforce from the same AWS Access Portal
Employees must use a separate Salesforce SSO portal — Identity Center does not support non-AWS applications
This requires configuring an Amazon Cognito User Pool to broker between Identity Center and Salesforce
quiz.explanation: AWS IAM Identity Center supports SAML 2.0 integrations with business cloud applications beyond just AWS accounts. From the same AWS Access Portal, employees can access SAML-enabled SaaS applications like Salesforce, Slack, Box, and many others — in addition to their AWS accounts. Identity Center acts as the central access hub for both cloud infrastructure (AWS accounts) and SaaS applications, providing a unified SSO experience.

quiz.question: A company uses AWS IAM Identity Center with AWS Managed Microsoft AD as the identity source. An employee is added to the 'Developers' Active Directory group. The 'Developers' group has an account assignment in Identity Center with the 'DeveloperAccess' permission set for the Development account. When does the employee gain access to the Development account?
options: Immediately — once added to the AD group, the employee has access within seconds
After the next scheduled Identity Center AD sync cycle completes and the group membership is updated in Identity Center
Only after an Identity Center administrator manually approves the access request
After the employee creates an IAM user in the Development account
quiz.explanation: When IAM Identity Center uses an external directory (like AWS Managed Microsoft AD) as the identity source, user and group information is synchronized from the directory to Identity Center. Group membership changes in AD propagate to Identity Center during the sync cycle. The account assignments (Developers group → DeveloperAccess → Development account) are already defined — once the sync reflects the new group membership, the employee will see the Development account in their Access Portal. There's no manual approval step unless you add it. No IAM users are created in the target account.

quiz.question: An architect is documenting the differences between AWS IAM Identity Center permission sets and traditional IAM roles. Which statement CORRECTLY describes a permission set?
options: A permission set is an IAM user created by Identity Center in each target account
A permission set is a collection of IAM policies that Identity Center provisions as an IAM role in each AWS account it is assigned to
A permission set is an organization-level IAM role that spans all AWS accounts simultaneously
A permission set is a session policy attached when users assume cross-account roles
quiz.explanation: A permission set in IAM Identity Center is a collection of IAM policies (similar to how you'd define an IAM role's permissions). When a permission set is assigned to an account, Identity Center automatically creates and manages an IAM role in that account with those policies. When a user assumes that permission set for an account, they're actually assuming the IAM role provisioned by Identity Center in that account via STS. Permission sets enable reuse — the same permission set can be provisioned as a role in multiple accounts. Users never get IAM users (Option A is wrong). Permission sets are account-specific, not organization-spanning (Option C is wrong).

quiz.question: A cloud team is designing the identity and access management strategy for a 30-account AWS Organization. Which TWO AWS-recommended practices should they follow for managing human access to AWS accounts? (Choose TWO)
options: Create IAM users in every account for each team member and share credentials via a secure password manager
Use AWS IAM Identity Center as the centralized solution for SSO and multi-account access management
Configure permission sets in Identity Center that follow the principle of least privilege for each team's function
Grant all team members AdministratorAccess in all accounts to reduce support tickets about permissions
Store permanent IAM access keys in AWS Secrets Manager for each team member to use
quiz.explanation: AWS IAM Identity Center is the recommended centralized solution for multi-account access (Option B). Configuring permission sets with least-privilege policies ensures each team only has the permissions they need (Option C — principle of least privilege is a security best practice). Creating IAM users in every account is exactly what Identity Center eliminates (Option A is wrong). AdministratorAccess for everyone violates least privilege (Option D is wrong). Storing permanent access keys — even in Secrets Manager — creates long-term credential risk; Identity Center uses temporary credentials automatically (Option E is wrong).

quiz.question: A developer leaves the company. The company uses AWS IAM Identity Center with Azure AD as the identity source. The IT team disables the developer's Azure AD account. What is the impact on the developer's AWS access?
options: The developer retains AWS access until an IAM administrator manually removes them from Identity Center
The developer loses access to the AWS Access Portal immediately because Identity Center authenticates users via Azure AD — a disabled Azure AD account cannot obtain a SAML token
The developer retains temporary AWS credentials already issued until they expire, and cannot get new ones
Both B and C are correct — they cannot log in via the portal, and any active sessions remain valid until they naturally expire
quiz.explanation: When Azure AD is the identity source, users authenticate at Azure AD to get a SAML token for Identity Center. Disabling the Azure AD account prevents the user from authenticating at Azure AD — they cannot obtain a new SAML token, so they cannot log into the AWS Access Portal or get new temporary credentials (making Option B correct). However, any temporary credentials already issued via STS that haven't expired yet will remain valid until their TTL expires (typically 1-8 hours). Both effects are correct: no new access possible (blocked at IdP), but active sessions complete naturally.
